name: Megalithic Circuit of Évora
name_original: Rota Megalítica — percurso circular na Serra de Monfurado
description: Hike the Megalithic Route in Serra de Monfurado, a difficult 12.1-mile trail exploring ancient monuments and scenic Alentejo landscapes.
region_intro: Hiking trails around Nossa Senhora Da Tourega traverse a landscape characterized by gently rolling plains and ancient historical sites within the Alentejo region of Portugal. The area features iconic cork oak forests, olive groves, and rural farmland, providing a serene backdrop for exploration. Hikers can expect to encounter significant megalithic monuments and Roman ruins, often situated amidst meadows and open countryside. This region offers opportunities to combine cultural discovery with natural beauty. Best hiking trails around Nossa Senhora Da Tourega The most popular hiking route is Megalithic Circuit of Évora, a 12.1 miles (19.4 km) trail that takes 5 hours 17 minutes to complete, exploring significant ancient structures. Another top favourite among local hikers is Almendres Menhir – Almendres Cromlech loop from Guadalupe, a moderate 5.8 miles (9.4 km) path through historic megalithic sites. Local hikers also love the Almendres Cromlech loop from Almendres Cromlech, a 3.1 miles (5.0 km) trail leading through ancient megalithic complexes and rural landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 21 minutes. Hiking around Nossa Senhora Da Tourega is defined by ancient monuments, open plains, and rural paths through cork oak forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to more challenging routes. The routes in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 700 hikers have used komoot to explore Nossa Senhora Da Tourega's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega?
A: There are over 35 hiking trails around Nossa Senhora Da Tourega, offering a variety of options for different skill levels and interests. The region is known for its gentle terrain and rich historical sites.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega?
A: Yes, Nossa Senhora Da Tourega offers many easy trails perfect for beginners. Out of the 38 available routes, 26 are classified as easy. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21931324" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Giraldo Square – Roman Temple of Évora loop from Évora&lt;/a&gt;, which is 3.6 miles (5.8 km) long and takes about 1 hour 34 minutes to complete.

Q: Can I find family-friendly hikes in the area?
A: Many of the easy trails in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega are suitable for families, featuring relatively flat terrain and opportunities to explore historical sites. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14655881" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barahona Palace – Giraldo Square loop from Évora&lt;/a&gt; is a good option, covering 3.2 miles (5.1 km) and passing through urban and historical landscapes.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Nossa Senhora Da Tourega?
A: Yes, many routes in the region are designed as circular walks, allowing you to start and end at the same point. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14655697" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almendres Menhir – Almendres Cromlech loop from Guadalupe&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate circular trail, spanning 5.8 miles (9.4 km) and exploring significant megalithic sites.

Q: What kind of historical sites or natural monuments can I see while hiking?
A: The hiking trails around Nossa Senhora Da Tourega are rich in history and natural beauty. You can encounter ancient megalithic monuments like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/764918" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almendres Cromlech&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/764900" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almendres Menhir&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7264295" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Great Dolmen of Zambujeiro&lt;/a&gt; and the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7201211" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Great Cork Oak&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are the hiking trails in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega dog-friendly?
A: Most trails in the rural and natural areas around Nossa Senhora Da Tourega are generally dog-friendly, especially if your dog is well-behaved and kept on a leash. Always ensure you carry enough water for both yourself and your pet, particularly during warmer months, as shade can be limited on open plains.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega?
A: The best time to hike in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega is typically during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are milder, and the landscape is often greener. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, while winters are generally mild but can be wet.

Q: Are there options for parking near the trailheads?
A: Yes, for many of the popular trails, especially those starting near towns or major attractions, you'll find designated parking areas. For more remote routes, roadside parking might be available, but it's always advisable to check specific route details on komoot for parking suggestions before you go.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega?
A: The hiking trails in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 100 reviews. Hikers often praise the tranquil Alentejo environment, the historical significance of the megalithic sites, and the well-maintained paths that offer a peaceful escape into nature.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging hikes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for those seeking a more challenging experience, Nossa Senhora Da Tourega offers a few difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2255319783" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Megalithic Circuit of Évora&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 12.1 miles (19.4 km) trail that takes approximately 5 hours 17 minutes to complete, exploring significant ancient structures across varied terrain.

Q: Can I access hiking trails using public transport in Nossa Senhora Da Tourega?
A: While some trails, particularly those starting or ending in Évora, might be accessible via local bus services, many of the more rural or megalithic sites are best reached by car. It's recommended to check local public transport schedules if you plan to rely on it for trailhead access.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to eat near the hiking trails?
A: You'll find cafes and restaurants primarily in the nearby towns and villages, such as Évora. While some trails might pass close to smaller hamlets with local eateries, it's generally a good idea to pack snacks and water, especially for longer hikes in more remote areas, and plan to dine in a larger settlement before or after your hike.
tips:
It is one of the largest dolmens on the Iberian Peninsula. It was built between approximately 4000 B.C. and 3500 B.C. It was used as a cemetery and perhaps also for religious purposes
Source wikimedia

The stone, a menhir approximately 3.50 m high, is made of porphyritic granodiorite and, like many menhirs and most cylindrical limestone idols in the Alentejo, has one flat side and one curved side. It bears a báculo decoration, partially covered by an older, barely recognizable wavy line.

The remains of the Almendres dolmen can also be found nearby.

The Cromeleque consists of 3 stone circles. It dates back to the transitional period from the Stone Age to the Copper Age, i.e. from 4000 to 2800 BC. 1000 to 2000 years older than Stonehenge. It consists of about 100 one to three meter high stones. Some of them have rolled away and are no longer set up.

The site in Nossa Senhora da Tourega e Nossa Senhora de Guadalupe has been continuously occupied since 3000 B.C. and 1000 A.D. It was founded on a castro fortified proto-historic structure, with its origin in the Bronze Age, Chalcolithic, with vestiges from older settlements.

During the Middle Ages it was sporadically re-occupied and reconstructed.

Records from the 15th century indicate that the castle was associated with Giraldo Sem Pavor, a warrior who conquered Évora from the Muslims in 1165.

Today the site is being analyzed for classification.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of the Megalithic Route?
faqs.answer: The Megalithic Route is generally considered to be of low to moderate difficulty. While some sections have easily-accessible paths, the full circular route, which is about 19.4 km with an elevation gain of 279m, is rated as difficult and requires very good fitness. Be prepared for varied terrain, including rural paths that can be wet after rain.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the trail?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in prehistoric monuments. Key sights include the Almendres Cromlech, often called the "Portuguese Stonehenge," the impressive Almendres Menhir, and the Great Dolmen of Zambujeiro, one of the largest burial structures in the Iberian Peninsula. You'll also enjoy views of cork tree plantations, rolling plains, and the Alentejo region.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: Specific parking information for the trailhead is not detailed in the available data. However, given the trail's location near Évora and its popularity, it's advisable to look for designated parking areas near the starting points of the megalithic complexes, such as those serving the Almendres Cromlech or the Great Dolmen of Zambujeiro. Arriving early is often a good strategy, especially during peak seasons.

faqs.question: Is the Megalithic Route suitable for dogs?
faqs.answer: While specific regulations for dogs on this trail are not provided, many outdoor trails in Portugal are dog-friendly. It is generally recommended to keep dogs on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers and the historical sites. Always carry water for your dog and clean up after them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike the Megalithic Route is typically during the spring (March to May) or autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder and more pleasant for hiking, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the potentially heavy rains of winter. Spring also brings blooming wildflowers, adding to the scenic beauty.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee to hike the trail?
faqs.answer: There is no information indicating that permits or entrance fees are required to hike the Megalithic Route itself. Access to the natural areas and the megalithic sites along the trail is generally free. However, always check local regulations before your visit, especially for any specific site within the route that might have visitor centers or guided tours.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain should I expect on the Megalithic Route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied, featuring oak tree forests, open fields, and rural paths. While generally considered low difficulty, some sections might require sure-footedness. Be aware that parts of the trail can become inundated after rainfall, requiring hikers to navigate around water. Sturdy hiking shoes are recommended.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the full circular route?
faqs.answer: The full circular route, which covers approximately 19.4 km, typically takes around 5 hours and 17 minutes to complete. This duration can vary depending on your pace, the number of stops you make to explore the megalithic sites, and your overall fitness level.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like restrooms or water sources along the trail?
faqs.answer: Information about specific facilities like restrooms or dedicated water sources directly on the trail is not available. As this is a rural hiking route, it's best to assume limited facilities. Plan to carry sufficient water for the entire hike and use facilities in nearby towns or at the start/end points of the trail.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Acesso ao Menir trail, covering approximately 4% of its length. This can offer opportunities to explore additional paths in the area if you wish to extend your hike.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a hike on the Megalithic Route?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's length and varied terrain, you should bring sturdy hiking boots, plenty of water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and appropriate clothing layers. A map or GPS device is also recommended, especially since some sections might be less clearly marked. If hiking after rain, consider waterproof footwear.
